text: The talk of {site} tonight, delivered by a patron who swears they were there: "{rumor}"

choices.text: Drink it in. History is free

outcomes.text: Tonight the rumor cannot compete with the war outside the walls. The teller keeps glancing at the door mid-sentence, and every story bends toward the fighting whether it started there or not. You listen anyway. Someone has to remember the small things.
You commit it to memory, slightly wrong, continuing a proud oral tradition.

The teller clocks your thin purse before the barkeep can pour and waves your coin away. 'Story tax runs the other way for the broke,' they say, and buy YOUR round instead. The ending, told for free, is better than anything money gets.
Lubricated, they add details: names, dates, a startling amount of slander. Someone slips you a coin for the entertainment.
The second round undoes them entirely. The story dissolves into weeping about a goose. You'll never know the ending.

You declare it never happened, and because it is YOU saying so, the rumor dies on the spot. The teller deflates. By morning the story is officially untrue everywhere, and you are in it anyway, as the ending.
The tavern turns on you. Historical revisionism is a bar-fight offense here.
An actual historian in the corner backs you up, citing sources. The teller buys YOU a drink out of shame.

text: A hooded figure at {site} slides you a drink. It is fizzing. Drinks here don't fizz.

outcomes.text: The fizz meets your curse and, briefly, they cancel out. For one gleaming hour nothing goes wrong at all. Then the bubbles fade and the universe resumes billing, but you remember what quiet felt like.
It's just soda. From space, apparently. Weirdly invigorating.
Poison. Classic. You spend an hour befriending the floor.
You can now taste colors. Purple is the worst one.
They vanish in a puff of embarrassment, leaving their coin purse.
You step out into the rain, and behind you the gutters fizz where the drops land. Tonight everything fizzes. You put your hood up and walk considerably faster than dignity allows.
Coward. Effective, living coward.

text: A stone golem sits at the corner table of {site}, arm up, palm open. The chalkboard reads: 'BEAT GRUNDLE. WIN 20 GOLD. NO REFUNDS ON BONES.'

success.text: Grundle's arm falls. Your companion's victory scream shatters two mugs and one social norm, and the bar chants your name in shifts. The payout comes with a deduction for the mugs, which is fair.
Grundle's arm hits the table with a sound like a landslide. The bar goes silent, then erupts. Grundle nods, respectfully.

fail.text: Your arm loses decisively, but your companion catches you before you hit the floor and glares at Grundle until the golem apologizes. In gravel. It helps more than the bandages do.
Your arm makes a noise arms should not make.
You lose instantly, but with such style that someone buys you dinner.

choices.text: Bet on the next challenger instead

outcomes.text: The blacksmith beats Grundle on a technicality. Your bet pays out.
Grundle wins. Grundle always wins. The chalkboard was a warning, not a challenge.
Golems don't drink. Grundle holds the mug anyway, touched. You've made a very heavy friend.
You spend the evening barking odds and collecting a promoter's cut. Grundle wins every match. Grundle always wins. You knew that going in, which is the entire business model.
The house already HAS a promoter, who explains the concept of territory using a chair.

text: Tonight's special at {site} is 'Stew.' No further details. The bowl is looking at you.

success.text: Delicious, restorative, and only mildly sentient. You feel great and vaguely guilty.

fail.text: The stew wins. You spend the night negotiating with your own stomach.

outcomes.text: 'Stew,' says the cook. Then, quieter: 'Boiled twice. Plague rules.' It is the most reassuring sentence anyone has said to you all week. You eat. It is, if nothing else, extremely boiled.
The cook stares at you for a long time. 'Stew,' they say. You decide not to eat the stew. Wise.
The cook is so impressed someone finally asked that dinner is free.
The bread arrives warm, which in deep winter promotes it to the finest meal in the province. You hold it a while before eating, like a small rented hearth.
The bread is fine. The bread is always fine. Boring, but fine.

choices.text: Produce your own jar of stew and propose a taste-off

outcomes.text: The cook tries yours, goes very still, and offers coin and dinner for the recipe. You invent a recipe on the spot. Everyone wins except the truth.
The two stews should never have been introduced. Something curdles at a distance. You are asked to leave and to take 'that' with you.

text: A bard at {site} is singing a ballad about your life. The facts are wrong, the rhymes are worse, and the crowd loves it.

success.text: You bow, and the room realizes the ballad is TRUE and its subject is HERE. The bard nearly swallows a lute string. The take doubles, and the barkeep names the good chair after you.
You bow. The crowd roars. Hats fill with coins, and one of the hats is yours.

fail.text: You bow. A chair leg is thrown. History will remember the ballad, not the truth.

outcomes.text: The bard incorporates your corrections into a new, crueler verse. It's catchy.
The bard hires you as a fact-checker on the spot. Easiest coin you've ever made.
You slip out mid-verse, which is wise, because verse four rhymes 'reward' with 'sword' and half the room has started doing arithmetic. The back alley has never looked lovelier.
You escape. Behind you, the ballad gains a verse about your mysterious vanishing. Cool points, technically.

text: The barkeep of {site} slides you a tab as long as your arm. You ordered one drink. One item reads 'ambiance: 6 gold.'

success.text: The barkeep reads the name on the tab, goes pale, and blames the ambiance charge on 'a new hire.' Everything is comped, including, mysteriously, the drink. Reputation: the only currency that compounds.
You argue the ambiance was, at best, adequate. The barkeep, a professional, respects the negotiation and comps everything but the drink.

fail.text: The barkeep adds a 'dispute processing fee.' You pay it out of exhausted admiration.

outcomes.text: You pay from a purse so fat the barkeep's professionalism audibly strains. By the time you finish your drink the tab has grown a 'wealth ambiance surcharge.' You pay that too. It is cheaper than the argument and funnier than the ambiance.
You pay in full with a flourish. Word spreads. By sundown, three taverns claim you drank there, and one has named a sandwich after you.

success.text: Out the window, over the fence, gone. Somewhere behind you the tab flutters to the floor like a defeated bird.

fail.text: The bouncer catches you by the collar mid-leap. The tab now includes 'window: 4 gold' and your dignity.

text: A cartographer slumps at the bar of {site}, surrounded by rolled maps and empty mugs. 'Treasure maps,' she says. 'Real ones. Eight gold. The fakes are eleven; better paper.'

outcomes.text: She hears you have a ship and sobers completely. 'Then you want THIS one.' It is marked with soundings, reefs, and a note reading 'trust the third island.' Same price. Better map.
She picks one seemingly at random, kisses it for luck, and hands it over with the solemnity of a state funeral.

success.text: Five gold and you have to listen to the story of her divorce. Worth it. Mostly.

fail.text: Mid-haggle, the ink on every map within arm's reach runs. Slightly. Only near you. She watches it happen, then charges full price plus a surcharge listed as 'whatever that was.'
She counter-haggles UP, citing 'emotional labor.' You pay nine before you understand what happened.

choices.text: Ask why the fakes cost more

outcomes.text: 'People trust expensive lies.' She taps her temple. You leave wiser and she orders another round on the strength of the lesson.

text: The corner table of {site} has gone quiet. That's {npc} - THE {npc}, {npc_trait} {npc_prof} of {npc_faction} - nursing a drink and a reputation.

choices.text: Buy them a drink and ask for the true story

outcomes.text: They accept the drink, sip it, and say without looking up: 'Light purse tonight? Funny thing - mine was light once too. Same night I met you.' The room goes quiet. The exit seems very far away.
They know your name before you give it. 'Sit,' they say, and for an hour you trade true stories like contraband. When the scars are compared, yours hold up. Half the room is quietly taking notes.
Two drinks in, the truth comes out: it was luck, mostly, and the ballads left out the running away. You feel better about your own odds.
They tell the story with full theatrical staging, using your drink as a prop. It's spent by the end. So are you. Magnificent.

success.text: You win. The tavern loses its mind. {npc} buys the room a round and announces your name like a herald. This will be a ballad.

fail.text: Your arm is a footnote in their legend now. They're gracious about it, which is worse.

choices.text: Pick their pocket. Legends carry legendary coin

success.text: You lift a purse heavy with story-money. You will never tell anyone how easy it was, because no one would believe you.

fail.text: They catch your wrist without turning around. '{npc_trait},' they say, as if reading your file. The bouncer provides the rest of the lesson.

text: A stranger at {site} is holding a portrait and a grudge. 'My grandparent, {gone}. Died in year {gone_year}. And do you know what the histories call it? A FOOTNOTE.'

outcomes.text: You offer condolences and the grief cracks wide open: with plague in the streets, the stranger has been rehearsing eulogies for the living. You sit with them a long while. They press their grandparent's bandages on you. 'Somebody should get the use.'
You listen to the whole family history. All of it. The stranger, unburdened, pays for your dinner and calls you 'the only decent soul left.'

choices.text: Claim you've heard great things about {gone}

success.text: You improvise three flattering anecdotes on the spot. The stranger weeps, then insists on giving you a family heirloom over your polite objections.

fail.text: You get the year wrong, the deeds wrong, and, impressively, the name wrong, mid-sentence. The portrait gets involved. Frames hurt.

choices.text: Point out that footnotes are still history

outcomes.text: The stranger blinks. 'Footnotes... are still history.' A tension generations deep dissolves over one round of drinks, which they buy.
Wrong crowd for nuance. You wear the drink they bought.

text: A dice game in the back of {site}. The stakes are five gold, the dice are 'house dice,' and the dealer's smile has too many teeth in it.

outcomes.text: Your companion stands behind your chair, arms folded, studying the dealer's hands with unsettling patience. The house dice roll honest for the first time in their careers. You win modest, clean money, which confuses everyone.
The dice like you tonight. The dealer's smile loses a tooth of confidence.
The house dice do house things. You watch your coins join their family behind the counter.

success.text: Your palm is quicker than their loaded pips. You win with THEIR crooked dice, which every code of honor agrees is beautiful.

fail.text: Caught. Worse: recognized. The dealer glances at the poster behind the bar and names a price for forgetting your face. You pay it. The table suddenly remembers other appointments.
Caught. The house takes your stake plus a 'cheater's tithe,' and everyone gets to watch.

outcomes.text: You clock the loaded dice, the sleeve, and the mirror in the lamp. An education, free of charge.

choices.text: Produce your own dice and insist on neutral equipment

outcomes.text: Your dice, their table, everyone watching everyone. The game turns honest and you turn out to be good at it. The dealer respects you the way sharks respect other sharks.
The dealer agrees, swaps your dice back out with a sleight so fine it is practically legal, and beats you with your own equipment. You keep the dice and the lesson.

text: It's open-verse night at {site}. The bard's lute is passed from table to table like a dare. It is now in front of you. The room waits.

success.text: They came for open-verse night. They got YOU. The room sings the chorus back with your name in it before you have taught it to them. The tip hat requires a second hat.
You bring the house down with a ballad about a goblin toll collector and his rock. Coins rain. The barkeep declares your tab 'historical fiction.'

fail.text: You forget the words to a song you invented on the spot. The silence has texture. The lute is gently confiscated.

choices.text: Play something instrumental. No words, no risk

outcomes.text: You play to the rhythm of the rain on the roof, and the rain, flattered, keeps time. The whole tavern goes quiet and warm at once. Nobody tips a moment like that. Somebody tips anyway.
Wordless and haunting. Nobody can mock lyrics that don't exist. A tactician's performance, modestly tipped.

choices.text: Pass the lute and buy the next performer a drink

outcomes.text: The next performer, emboldened by your drink, is magnificent. You are remembered as part of the story. Adjacent glory still counts.

text: Behind the bar of {site}, a keg is chained shut. Not locked. CHAINED. The barkeep follows your gaze. 'House special. Waiver's under the till.'

choices.text: Sign the waiver. Drink the special

success.text: It goes down like a furnace deciding to be your friend. The winter outside stops mattering for a full day. The barkeep nods: 'That's why we chain it in summer.'
It tastes like lightning that's been through something. You remain standing. The bar applauds. The barkeep frames the waiver, unneeded.

fail.text: You wake up two hours later under a table you don't recognize, holding a certificate that says 'PARTICIPANT.'

outcomes.text: Clause four covers 'temporary translucency.' Clause nine is written in a language that hurts. You learn everything you need to know without drinking anything.

'War ration,' says the barkeep, patting the keg. 'A quartermaster left it as payment last war. We added the chain after what it did to the quartermaster.' You order water and drink to the quartermaster.
The barkeep leans in: 'The last three people who asked are the reason for the chain.' You order water. The water is excellent.
